content.text: Contract test CI integration is the practice of running consumer-driven contract tests automatically inside continuous integration (CI) pipelines so that incompatibilities between API consumers and providers are caught before any service is deployed. Rather than relying on shared staging environments or manual coordination, each service team owns its own CI pipeline, and a central
acts as the single source of truth for published contracts (pacts) and their verification results.

In a typical setup, the consumer's CI generates a pact file and publishes it to the broker whenever a pull request is opened. The broker then notifies the provider's CI via a webhook. The provider pulls the latest pacts, runs verification against its own API implementation, and publishes the result back to the broker. A final

query to the broker acts as the deployment gate: it checks whether the exact version of the consumer and the exact version of the provider are mutually compatible for a target environment before allowing either service to proceed to production.

library, the Pact Broker (self-hosted open-source) or PactFlow (SaaS), and CI integrations for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins — provides all the tooling needed to implement this workflow without building custom coordination infrastructure.

Microservice and agentic AI architectures are composed of independently deployed services that communicate over HTTP or message queues. A breaking change — renaming a field, changing a status code, altering an enum value — made by the provider team may go undetected until it crashes a consumer in production. Traditional end-to-end tests in shared staging environments catch these breaks, but they are slow, flaky, and require all services to be deployed simultaneously, which negates the independence that microservices are supposed to provide.

Contract tests embedded in CI pipelines solve this by making compatibility verification a first-class, automated step in each team's workflow. When a provider developer changes the API, the verification job immediately tells them whether that change breaks any consumer. When a consumer developer changes what they expect from a provider, the pact is republished and the provider's CI runs verification automatically within minutes of the consumer's PR being opened. The feedback loop tightens from days (manual testing) or hours (shared staging) to minutes.

For agentic AI systems specifically — where an orchestrator agent calls LLM gateways, tool servers, and retrieval services — this pattern prevents silent regressions in the JSON schemas exchanged between components, ensuring that changes to tool call response formats or function signatures are caught before they cause agents to fail at runtime.
Contract test execution is sub-second; consumer CI jobs that generate pacts typically complete in under two minutes. Provider verification runs add comparable time to the provider pipeline.
Each service can be deployed independently as long as can-i-deploy confirms compatibility. Teams do not need to coordinate release windows or maintain shared staging.

The Pact Broker's web UI shows which consumers depend on which providers, what each interaction expects, and the current verification status per environment. This replaces manually maintained API docs.

Pacts are generated from consumer test code, not from provider documentation. This shifts API design to be driven by actual consumer needs, preventing providers from exposing fields or endpoints that no consumer uses.

The broker tracks which version of each service is deployed to staging, production, or any custom environment. can-i-deploy queries target a specific environment, ensuring the compatibility check reflects reality.
Contract tests run against mocked or real provider implementations in isolation, removing the need to keep a full service mesh running solely for integration testing.

Contract tests verify the shape and semantics of interactions, not business logic. A provider that returns correctly-shaped responses with wrong data will pass verification. Contract testing is not a substitute for integration tests that confirm end-to-end correctness of a feature — it is a complement that focuses on the contract boundary.

The broker adds operational overhead. A self-hosted Pact Broker requires database maintenance, upgrades, and uptime monitoring. PactFlow eliminates this but introduces a SaaS dependency and licensing cost. Either way, the broker becomes a critical piece of infrastructure: if it is unavailable, contract publishing and can-i-deploy queries fail, potentially blocking all deployments.
Wiring consumer CI, provider CI, the broker, and webhooks together requires coordination across teams and CI configuration changes in multiple repositories.
Verification runs the provider's real service or a test instance of it, which may take several minutes. This adds to the consumer PR's wait time for the end-to-end green signal.

When a consumer publishes a new pact that the provider has not yet verified, the provider CI will fail on that pact by default. The pending pact feature prevents this but requires careful broker configuration.
Standard Pact verification covers synchronous HTTP. Testing Kafka or other message-broker interactions requires the pact-message plugin and additional setup.

The workflow has five actors: the consumer repository, the provider repository, the Pact Broker, the consumer's CI system, and the provider's CI system. A single PR on the consumer side triggers a chain of events across all five.

tests exercise the consumer's client code against a mock provider started by the Pact library. At the end of each successful test run, the library writes a pact file — a JSON document recording the exact HTTP interactions the consumer expects. The CI job then publishes this pact to the broker using the
command (or the Python API), tagging it with the consumer version (typically the git SHA) and the branch name.

The broker's webhook fires a POST request to the provider's CI triggering a new pipeline run. The provider's CI fetches pacts from the broker (all pacts marked for verification for that provider), starts the provider's service in test mode, and runs the Pact verifier against it. The verifier replays each recorded interaction against the real provider and checks that the responses match. Results — pass or fail, per interaction — are published back to the broker. When the consumer's CI then queries
for the environment, the broker checks its matrix of consumer versions versus provider versions and returns a pass or fail verdict.
The diagram traces the full lifecycle. A consumer PR triggers the
which runs pact-python tests and publishes the generated pact to the
. The broker fires a webhook to the
, which fetches the pacts, verifies them, and publishes results back to the broker. The
runs can-i-deploy against the broker; a passing result allows deployment to Production, while a failure blocks the deploy.

Contract test CI integration is most valuable when two or more independently deployable services exchange data over HTTP and both teams want to deploy on their own schedule without requiring the other team to be available for manual testing. It becomes essential when the number of consumer-provider pairs grows beyond three or four, making manual compatibility tracking impractical.
you have multiple consumers depending on a single provider API and breaking changes to the provider are frequent.
teams are geographically or organizationally separated and synchronous coordination for releases is expensive.
an agentic orchestrator calls multiple tool servers with structured JSON schemas and you need to ensure schema changes do not silently break the orchestrator.
the consumer and provider are always deployed together as a monolith — contract testing overhead is not justified if you cannot deploy them independently.
you consume a third-party API you do not control — the provider will never run pact verification, so only consumer-side tests (without broker verification) are possible.
Deploy a Pact Broker. Use the official Docker image (
) or sign up for PactFlow. Note the broker URL and generate an API token.
. Each test exercises the consumer's HTTP client against a mock provider and records the interaction.
Add a publish step to the consumer CI pipeline. After tests pass, run
set to the git SHA and
Configure a webhook in the Pact Broker UI or API to POST to the provider's CI trigger URL whenever a new pact is published.
Write provider verification tests using pact-python's
class. The test fetches pacts from the broker by provider name, starts the provider service, and verifies each interaction.
as the final step before every deployment. Query the broker for the pacticipant name, version, and target environment. Exit non-zero if the result is not safe to deploy.
Record deployments after a successful deploy by calling
so the broker knows which version is in which environment.

The ordering matters: the consumer CI must publish before the provider CI verifies, and the broker must record the deployed version before can-i-deploy queries will reflect environment state accurately.

# .github/workflows/consumer-ci.yml
name: Consumer CI

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
  push:
    branches: [main]

env:
  PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL: https://broker.example.com
  PACT_BROKER_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_TOKEN }}

jobs:
  test-and-publish-pact:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install pact-python pytest requests

      - name: Run consumer pact tests
        run: pytest tests/pact/ -v
        # pact-python writes pact files to ./pacts/ by default

      - name: Publish pact to broker
        run: |
          pip install pact-foundation-cli
          pact-broker publish ./pacts \
            --consumer-app-version ${{ github.sha }} \
            --branch ${{ github.head_ref || github.ref_name }} \
            --broker-base-url $PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL \
            --broker-token $PACT_BROKER_TOKEN

      - name: Can I deploy to production?
        # Only run on the main branch before deployment
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        run: |
          pact-broker can-i-deploy \
            --pacticipant OrchestrationService \
            --version ${{ github.sha }} \
            --to-environment production \
            --broker-base-url $PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL \
            --broker-token $PACT_BROKER_TOKEN

      - name: Deploy
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        run: ./scripts/deploy.sh production

      - name: Record deployment
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        run: |
          pact-broker record-deployment \
            --pacticipant OrchestrationService \
            --version ${{ github.sha }} \
            --environment production \
            --broker-base-url $PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL \
            --broker-token $PACT_BROKER_TOKEN

# .github/workflows/provider-ci.yml
name: Provider CI

on:
  # Triggered by the Pact Broker webhook when a new pact is published
  repository_dispatch:
    types: [pact-changed]
  # Also runs on normal pushes to main
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

env:
  PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL: https://broker.example.com
  PACT_BROKER_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_TOKEN }}

jobs:
  verify-pacts:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install pact-python pytest fastapi uvicorn

      - name: Start provider service
        run: |
          uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 &
          sleep 3  # wait for startup

      - name: Run provider pact verification
        env:
          PROVIDER_VERSION: ${{ github.sha }}
          PROVIDER_BRANCH: ${{ github.head_ref || github.ref_name }}
        run: pytest tests/pact/test_provider_verification.py -v

      - name: Can I deploy provider to production?
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        run: |
          pact-broker can-i-deploy \
            --pacticipant LLMGatewayService \
            --version ${{ github.sha }} \
            --to-environment production \
            --broker-base-url $PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL \
            --broker-token $PACT_BROKER_TOKEN

      - name: Deploy provider
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        run: ./scripts/deploy.sh production

      - name: Record provider deployment
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        run: |
          pact-broker record-deployment \
            --pacticipant LLMGatewayService \
            --version ${{ github.sha }} \
            --environment production \
            --broker-base-url $PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL \
            --broker-token $PACT_BROKER_TOKEN

# tests/pact/test_llm_gateway_consumer.py
# Consumer: OrchestrationService
# Provider: LLMGatewayService
# pip install pact-python pytest requests

import json
import pytest
import requests
from pact import Consumer, Provider, Like, EachLike, Term

PACT_DIR = "./pacts"
MOCK_HOST = "localhost"
MOCK_PORT = 1234


@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def pact():
    """Set up a Pact mock provider for the LLM Gateway."""
    pact = Consumer("OrchestrationService").has_pact_with(
        Provider("LLMGatewayService"),
        host_name=MOCK_HOST,
        port=MOCK_PORT,
        pact_dir=PACT_DIR,
        log_dir="./logs",
    )
    pact.start_service()
    yield pact
    pact.stop_service()


def test_complete_chat_returns_assistant_message(pact):
    """Orchestrator expects the LLM gateway to return a chat completion."""
    expected_body = {
        "id": Like("chatcmpl-abc123"),
        "object": "chat.completion",
        "choices": EachLike({
            "index": 0,
            "message": {
                "role": "assistant",
                "content": Like("The answer is 42."),
            },
            "finish_reason": Term(r"stop|length|tool_calls", "stop"),
        }),
        "usage": {
            "prompt_tokens": Like(10),
            "completion_tokens": Like(20),
            "total_tokens": Like(30),
        },
    }

    (
        pact
        .given("the LLM gateway is available")
        .upon_receiving("a chat completion request")
        .with_request(
            method="POST",
            path="/v1/chat/completions",
            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
            body={
                "model": "gpt-4o-mini",
                "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is the answer?"}],
            },
        )
        .will_respond_with(200, body=expected_body)
    )

    with pact:
        response = requests.post(
            f"http://{MOCK_HOST}:{MOCK_PORT}/v1/chat/completions",
            json={
                "model": "gpt-4o-mini",
                "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is the answer?"}],
            },
            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        )

    assert response.status_code == 200
    data = response.json()
    assert data["object"] == "chat.completion"
    assert len(data["choices"]) > 0
    assert data["choices"][0]["message"]["role"] == "assistant"

# tests/pact/test_provider_verification.py
# Provider: LLMGatewayService
# Fetches pacts from the Pact Broker and verifies the running service

import os
import pytest
from pact import Verifier

BROKER_URL = os.environ["PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL"]
BROKER_TOKEN = os.environ["PACT_BROKER_TOKEN"]
PROVIDER_VERSION = os.environ.get("PROVIDER_VERSION", "local")
PROVIDER_BRANCH = os.environ.get("PROVIDER_BRANCH", "main")
PROVIDER_BASE_URL = "http://localhost:8000"


def test_provider_honours_pacts_from_broker():
    """
    Fetches all consumer pacts for LLMGatewayService from the broker and
    verifies that the running provider satisfies each recorded interaction.
    """
    verifier = Verifier(
        provider="LLMGatewayService",
        provider_base_url=PROVIDER_BASE_URL,
    )

    output, _ = verifier.verify_with_broker(
        broker_url=BROKER_URL,
        broker_token=BROKER_TOKEN,
        # Fetch pacts for consumers on the main branch and
        # all pacts deployed to any environment
        consumer_version_selectors=[
            {"mainBranch": True},
            {"deployedOrReleased": True},
        ],
        # Enable pending pacts so new unverified pacts do not break CI
        enable_pending=True,
        # Include work-in-progress pacts from feature branches
        include_wip_pacts_since="2025-01-01",
        publish_verification_results=True,
        provider_app_version=PROVIDER_VERSION,
        provider_version_branch=PROVIDER_BRANCH,
        # State handlers let you set up provider state before each interaction
        provider_states_setup_url=f"{PROVIDER_BASE_URL}/_pact/provider-states",
    )

    # output is 0 when all verifications pass
    assert output == 0, "Pact verification failed — check broker for details"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# scripts/deploy.sh <environment>
# Guards the deploy with a can-i-deploy check against the Pact Broker.
set -euo pipefail

ENVIRONMENT=${1:-staging}
PACTICIPANT=${PACTICIPANT:-OrchestrationService}
VERSION=${GIT_SHA:-$(git rev-parse HEAD)}

echo "==> Checking can-i-deploy for $PACTICIPANT@$VERSION -> $ENVIRONMENT"
pact-broker can-i-deploy \
  --pacticipant "$PACTICIPANT" \
  --version "$VERSION" \
  --to-environment "$ENVIRONMENT" \
  --broker-base-url "$PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL" \
  --broker-token "$PACT_BROKER_TOKEN" \
  --retry-while-unknown 6 \
  --retry-interval 10
# --retry-while-unknown retries up to 6 times (10s apart) if the
# provider verification result is not yet available in the broker.

echo "==> can-i-deploy PASSED. Deploying $PACTICIPANT to $ENVIRONMENT ..."
# ... your actual deploy command here (kubectl, helm, etc.) ...

echo "==> Recording deployment"
pact-broker record-deployment \
  --pacticipant "$PACTICIPANT" \
  --version "$VERSION" \
  --environment "$ENVIRONMENT" \
  --broker-base-url "$PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL" \
  --broker-token "$PACT_BROKER_TOKEN"

echo "==> Done: $PACTICIPANT $VERSION deployed to $ENVIRONMENT"
Consider an agentic AI platform with three services: an
(proxies and rate-limits calls to the model API), and a
(executes tool calls and returns structured results). The orchestrator is the consumer of both the LLM gateway and the tool server. Both the LLM gateway and tool server are providers.
When the ToolServer team decides to rename the
field in its JSON response to

, they open a PR. Their CI runs unit tests and pushes the provider version to staging. Before the deploy gate runs, the broker's can-i-deploy check queries whether the OrchestrationService version currently deployed in staging is compatible with this new ToolServer version. Since the consumer's pact still expects the

field, verification fails and the ToolServer deploy is blocked. The broker's web UI shows the exact interaction that failed and which consumer version it affects, giving the ToolServer team a clear action item.

placeholders with the actual values from the event at fire time. The provider CI receives the consumer version and pact URL directly in the webhook payload, allowing it to verify the exact pact that triggered the webhook rather than all pacts in the broker.

Teams sometimes run contract tests and publish results but forget to add the can-i-deploy gate to the deployment pipeline. This means pacts are verified but the compatibility check never blocks a broken deploy. The can-i-deploy command must run as the last step before the actual deployment, not just once in a test phase.

Without a webhook from the broker to the provider CI, the provider only verifies pacts when its own CI runs — typically on provider code changes. A consumer that publishes a new pact will not trigger provider verification automatically, so the broker's can-i-deploy matrix stays incomplete for hours or days until the next provider CI run. Webhooks are not optional; they are what closes the feedback loop between consumer and provider pipelines.

By default, if a consumer publishes a pact for a new interaction that the provider has never verified, the provider CI will fail on that interaction even though the provider has not had a chance to implement it yet. This is correct behavior for existing interactions but creates friction for new ones under active development. Enabling the
flag in the Verifier call means that interactions that have never been verified successfully will not cause the provider CI to fail — they are pending — until the provider explicitly supports them.
rely on the broker knowing which versions of each service are currently deployed to production. If you never call
after a deploy, the broker has no environment state and can-i-deploy will either return an error or default to permissive behavior, defeating the purpose of the gate.
Tag consumer versions with branch and git SHA.
This lets the broker know which pact version corresponds to which code and enables consumer version selectors to fetch only the pacts relevant to the current provider branch.
Use consumer version selectors over tags.
are more reliable than environment tags for identifying which pacts the provider needs to verify.

clause in consumer tests maps to a provider state handler that seeds the correct data or state before the verifier replays the interaction. Without state handlers, verification fails for any interaction that requires specific data to be present.
When the consumer CI publishes a pact and immediately queries can-i-deploy, the provider verification may not have completed yet. The
flag polls the broker with a configurable interval rather than failing immediately.
Test only the fields the consumer actually uses, using Pact's
matchers for flexible matching. Overly strict pacts that assert on every field will break on innocuous provider additions.
Store the broker URL and token in CI secrets.
Never hardcode broker credentials in workflow files or committed code. Use GitHub Actions secrets, GitLab CI variables, or a secrets manager.
Run can-i-deploy in both consumer and provider pipelines.

Each service should gate its own deployment. Do not assume the other side's CI will catch all problems — both consumer and provider deployments should independently verify compatibility before proceeding.

Contract test execution is extremely fast. A single pact interaction test runs in milliseconds; even a suite of fifty consumer interactions for a complex provider typically completes in under 30 seconds. The dominant cost is starting the provider service for verification, which may take 10–60 seconds depending on framework warmup time.

As the number of consumer-provider pairs grows, the broker becomes the coordination bottleneck. A deployment by any consumer triggers provider CI runs, and if multiple consumers publish pacts simultaneously, the provider CI may receive a burst of webhook events. Most CI systems queue jobs and handle this gracefully, but monitoring queue depth and provider CI throughput becomes important at scale.
If a provider has many consumers, run verification of each consumer's pacts in separate parallel CI jobs rather than sequentially in one job.
Including work-in-progress pacts from all feature branches in every verification run adds overhead. Scope WIP inclusion to pacts published after a recent date to limit the blast radius.
Use Docker layer caching and dependency caching in CI to reduce provider service startup time from 60 seconds to under 10.
The Pact Broker retains all historical pact versions. Prune old versions with
on a schedule to prevent unbounded growth.
Consumer test authoring and provider verification
Wraps the Pact reference implementation; supports HTTP and async interactions
Central store for pacts and verification results
Self-hosted via Docker; PostgreSQL backend; open source under MIT
Managed Pact Broker with extra features
SaaS product by SmartBear; adds bidirectional contract testing, RBAC, audit logs
Available as a Docker image or Ruby gem; used in CI workflow YAML steps
repository_dispatch event used to trigger provider CI from broker webhooks
Pipeline triggers via API; environment variables set from broker webhook payload
Generic webhook trigger; parameters injected via the Pact Broker webhook body

The Pact broker approach uniquely combines consumer-driven contract authoring with automated cross-team verification and an explicit deployment gate. Shared integration suites and manual testing are simpler to start but do not scale as the number of service pairs grows. OpenAPI validation is provider-driven and does not encode what any specific consumer actually uses, making it less precise than Pact.

The Pact Broker is a critical piece of infrastructure: if it is down, publishing pacts and running can-i-deploy queries both fail, which may block all deployments. Run the broker with high-availability configuration (multiple replicas, a managed PostgreSQL database, and health checks) or use PactFlow's SaaS offering which provides an SLA.
Store the broker API token in your CI system's secret store, not in workflow files. Rotate tokens on a schedule and use separate read-only and read-write tokens where the broker supports it.
The broker can include a shared secret in webhook request headers. Verify this secret in the provider CI trigger endpoint to prevent unauthorized pipeline triggers.
Treat can-i-deploy failures as hard blocks.
Never add override flags that allow deployment to proceed despite a can-i-deploy failure except in documented emergency runbook scenarios. Every override is a potential production outage.

The broker's database contains the entire history of contracts and verification results. Losing it means losing the deployment gate state for all environments. Run daily backups and test restoration regularly.

Pact files record the exact HTTP interactions, including request and response bodies. If your service processes sensitive data (PII, authentication tokens), ensure that fixture data used in pacts is scrubbed or synthetic, and that broker access is restricted to authorised CI systems.

Contract test CI integration links consumer and provider pipelines through a Pact Broker: the consumer publishes a pact, the broker notifies the provider, the provider verifies, and can-i-deploy gates every deployment.
The can-i-deploy command is the most important step — without it, verified pacts provide no deployment protection. It must run as a hard gate, not a soft warning.

Webhooks from the broker to the provider CI are what make the feedback loop tight. Without them, the provider only verifies when its own code changes, leaving consumer pact changes unverified for hours.
pact-python's consumer tests use Pact matchers (
) to express flexible expectations, avoiding overly rigid assertions that would cause unnecessary provider failures.
Record deployments with pact-broker record-deployment after every successful deploy so the broker's environment state is accurate for subsequent can-i-deploy queries.
Enable pending pacts so that new consumer interactions under development do not break the provider CI before the provider has had a chance to implement the corresponding behaviour.

For agentic AI systems, contract testing at the tool server and LLM gateway boundaries prevents schema drift from silently breaking agent loops — catching JSON field renames, status code changes, and response structure changes at the PR stage rather than in production.
